articles.title: Room-by-Room Fall Prevention Checklist for Home Safety
articles.summary: Walk through each area of the home with this checklist to reduce tripping hazards and prevent falls.
articles.content:
Falls are one of the leading causes of injury for older adults, but many risks can be reduced with simple home changes. This room-by-room checklist helps you identify hazards and prioritize fixes.

Entryways:
- Ensure good lighting at all doors.
- Remove loose rugs or secure them with non-slip backing.
- Install handrails on steps.

Living areas:
- Keep walkways clear of cords and clutter.
- Arrange furniture to create wide, stable paths.
- Use non-slip pads under area rugs.

Bathroom:
- Add grab bars by the toilet and in the shower.
- Place a non-slip mat inside and outside the tub.
- Consider a raised toilet seat and shower chair.

Bedroom:
- Keep a lamp within easy reach of the bed.
- Use nightlights to light the path to the bathroom.
- Store frequently used items at waist height.

Stairs and hallways:
- Install sturdy handrails on both sides of stairs.
- Mark the edges of steps with contrasting tape if vision is limited.
- Repair loose or uneven steps promptly.

Periodic fall risk reviews, combined with support from a home care team, can significantly lower the chance of serious injury.

articles.title: Fall Prevention Tips for Seniors at Home
articles.summary: Practical fall prevention tips for older adults living independently at home.
articles.content:
Staying independent at home is possible with the right fall prevention strategies. This guide focuses on three key areas: environment, strength, and medications.

1. Environment:
- Keep floors dry and clean up spills immediately.
- Ensure all rooms and hallways are well lit.
- Use sturdy, supportive shoes instead of slippers or socks.

2. Strength and balance:
- Ask your doctor about safe exercises to improve balance.
- Consider tai chi or gentle yoga classes for seniors.
- Use mobility aids (cane, walker) as prescribed and keep them within reach.

3. Medications and health:
- Review medications regularly with your doctor or pharmacist.
- Have vision and hearing checked annually.
- Report dizziness or near-falls to your care team.

Home caregivers can assist with safe transfers, supervised walking, and environmental checks, further reducing risk.

articles.title: Fall Prevention After a Hospital Stay: What Families Should Know
articles.summary: Recovery after hospitalization is a high-risk time for falls. Learn how to plan ahead.
articles.content:
The weeks after a hospital stay are a high-risk period for falls due to weakness, new medications, and unfamiliar equipment.

Risk factors:
- Muscle loss from bed rest.
- New or changed medications that cause dizziness.
- Confusion or delirium in new environments.

Prevention strategies:
- Arrange for assistance with walking and transfers for the first few days at home.
- Keep frequently used items close to the bed or favorite chair.
- Review discharge instructions with a nurse or caregiver.
- Schedule follow-up appointments to reassess mobility and medications.

Professional in-home caregivers can provide standby assistance, help with bathing and dressing, and monitor for early warning signs of decline.

articles.title: Making the Bathroom Safer for Aging in Place
articles.summary: Simple upgrades can turn the bathroom from a high-risk space into a safer, more accessible room.
articles.content: Bathrooms are one of the most common locations for falls. This article explains grab bars, non-slip surfaces, and layout changes that lower risk and preserve dignity.

articles.title: Engaging Memory Activities for Loved Ones with Dementia
articles.summary: Meaningful activities can reduce agitation and support quality of life for people living with dementia.
articles.content: Structured, familiar activities can help people with dementia feel calmer and more connected. Try music from their youth, sorting tasks, or simple art projects, and avoid overwhelming choices.

articles.title: How to Communicate with a Loved One Living with Dementia
articles.summary: Learn simple communication techniques that reduce frustration for both you and your loved one.
articles.content: Use short sentences, yes/no questions, and a calm tone. Make eye contact, minimize background noise, and allow extra time for responses. Nonverbal cues like touch and facial expressions are often more important than words.

articles.title: What to Ask When Hiring a Home Caregiver
articles.summary: Key questions to ask agencies and individual caregivers before inviting them into your home.
articles.content: Before hiring a caregiver, ask about training, background checks, supervision, backup coverage, and how care plans are updated. Clarify hourly rates, minimum shifts, and cancellation policies.

articles.title: How Respite Care Helps Family Caregivers Recharge
articles.summary: Respite care gives family caregivers time to rest, attend appointments, and prevent burnout.
articles.content: Short-term respite visits can be scheduled weekly or as needed. They provide supervision, companionship, and assistance with daily activities while the primary caregiver takes a break.

articles.title: Safe Transportation to Medical Appointments for Seniors
articles.summary: Learn how caregivers can provide safe rides and support before, during, and after medical visits.
articles.content: Caregivers can assist with door-through-door transportation: helping with coats, mobility aids, transfers in and out of vehicles, and note-taking during appointments.

articles.title: Our Commitment to Safer Care at Home
articles.summary: Learn about our mission, values, and why safety is at the heart of our care model.
articles.content: Our agency was founded by nurses who saw the need for compassionate, reliable support that keeps seniors safe at home. We focus on rigorous caregiver training, fall prevention, and clear communication with families.

articles.title: Light Housekeeping Tasks Caregivers Can Safely Help With
articles.summary: Understand which household tasks are appropriate for non-medical home caregivers.
articles.content: Caregivers can usually assist with laundry, dishes, light meal prep, and tidying common areas. Heavy lifting, outdoor maintenance, and home repairs are typically outside of scope.

articles.title: How Medication Reminder Visits Work
articles.summary: See what to expect when a caregiver provides medication reminder support at home.
articles.content: Non-medical caregivers can remind clients to take pre-set medications, offer water or snacks, and note any side effects to report to the nurse or physician. They do not change dosages or administer injections.

articles.title: Creating a Safe Evening Routine for Loved Ones with Dementia
articles.summary: Reduce sundowning behaviors with a calming, predictable evening routine.
articles.content: Maintain consistent dinner and bedtime, reduce stimulation in the evening, and keep lighting soft but adequate to prevent falls or confusion.

articles.title: What to Expect from Post-Surgery Recovery Support at Home
articles.summary: Caregivers can provide essential support with mobility, daily tasks, and safety after surgery.
articles.content: Post-surgery caregivers assist with transfers, walking, bathing, light meal prep, and monitoring for concerning changes. They coordinate with your medical team and help track follow-up instructions.

articles.title: How to Build a Custom Weekly Home Care Schedule
articles.summary: Combine personal care, housekeeping, and companionship into a weekly routine that fits your life.
articles.content: Start with essential needs like bathing, dressing, and medications. Then add blocks for housekeeping and social visits. Review the schedule every few months and adjust as needs change.

caregivers.biography: Anna is a certified home health aide with 6 years of experience supporting older adults with bathing, dressing, and safe mobility in Manhattan.
Ben provides reliable personal care and companionship, specializing in morning routines and medication reminders.
Carla is an experienced caregiver with a gentle approach to personal care and light housekeeping for clients in Midtown.
David focuses on complex personal care and mobility support for clients with higher physical needs.
Elena is a dementia care specialist fluent in English and Spanish, known for her calm presence and excellent communication with families.
Francisco has over 9 years of experience supporting clients with dementia and providing meaningful companionship in Spanish.
Gabriela supports clients after surgery and those living with early-stage dementia, focusing on safety and fall prevention.
Henry is a registered nurse who provides specialized post-surgery recovery support and complex care coordination.
Inés combines post-operative support with light housekeeping to keep clients comfortable and safe at home.
Jamal focuses on companionship visits and community outings to reduce isolation.
Karen is experienced in dementia care and post-surgical support, with a special focus on communication with families.
Lucas is a bilingual caregiver with advanced dementia care training, supporting clients with complex behaviors.
Maria supports clients with personal care routines and light meal preparation, primarily in northern New Jersey.
Noah offers companionship visits, transportation to appointments, and light household help.
Olivia is a highly rated caregiver specializing in complex personal care and medication reminders.
Priya supports families with flexible hourly care, including personal care, light housekeeping, and companionship.

help_faqs.question: What number do I call after hours for urgent care needs?
help_faqs.answer: For urgent issues that cannot wait until our regular office hours, please call our after-hours emergency line at **1-800-555-0199**. This line is staffed 24/7 by an on-call coordinator who can reach a nurse or on-call supervisor as needed.

help_faqs.question: What should I do in a life-threatening emergency?
help_faqs.answer: If you believe someone is experiencing a life-threatening emergency (such as difficulty breathing, chest pain, or severe bleeding), call 911 immediately before contacting our agency. After you have called 911, you may notify our team at **1-800-555-0199** so we can coordinate follow-up care.

help_faqs.question: Can I request a same-day emergency visit?
help_faqs.answer: Same-day emergency visit requests are handled on a case-by-case basis. Call our office during business hours or our after-hours line at **1-800-555-0199**. We will review caregiver availability and the urgency of the situation to determine if we can send a caregiver the same day.

help_faqs.question: When will I be invoiced for services?
help_faqs.answer: Most clients receive invoices once per month for the prior months services. If you are on a monthly care plan, you may see a pre-bill for the upcoming month. Invoice dates and due dates are listed clearly on each invoice in your client portal.

help_faqs.question: What payment methods do you accept?
help_faqs.answer: You can pay invoices securely in the client portal using a saved credit or debit card (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, Discover) or a linked bank account. We also accept checks by mail. Cash payments are only accepted at our main office and must be accompanied by a receipt.

help_faqs.question: How long do I have to pay an invoice?
help_faqs.answer: Invoices are typically due 14 days after the issue date. The exact due date appears at the top of each invoice. Late payments may result in a temporary pause in services until the balance is resolved.

help_faqs.question: How do I book a one-time visit with a caregiver?
help_faqs.answer: To book a one-time visit, click the "Book a visit" option in the top navigation, choose your service type, enter your ZIP code, select a date and time window, and review available caregivers. Once you select a caregiver, you can submit a visit request directly from the booking form.

help_faqs.question: Can I set up recurring weekly visits?
help_faqs.answer: Yes. You can schedule recurring visits in two ways: by using the "Build your care plan" tool to create a weekly schedule, or by opening a caregivers profile and selecting "Request recurring visits." Our team will confirm the schedule and caregiver availability before finalizing.

help_faqs.question: What types of services do your caregivers provide?
help_faqs.answer: We offer non-medical home care services including personal care (bathing, dressing, grooming), mobility support, medication reminders, light housekeeping, companionship, dementia care, post-surgery recovery support, and escorted transportation to appointments. Service availability may vary by location and caregiver.

help_faqs.question: Do caregivers administer medications?
help_faqs.answer: Our caregivers provide medication reminders only. They can prompt clients to take medications that have been pre-set by the client, family, or nurse, and they can offer water or snacks. They do not change dosages, administer injections, or make medical decisions about medications.

help_faqs.question: How do I get started with home care services?
help_faqs.answer: Most families begin by requesting a free in-home assessment. During the visit, we review safety, daily routines, and care goals, then recommend a plan. You can request an assessment from the "Free in-home assessment" link in the top navigation or by calling our office.

help_faqs.question: Do you have caregivers who speak languages other than English?
help_faqs.answer: Yes. Many of our caregivers speak additional languages, including Spanish, Chinese, and French. On the "Find a caregiver" page, you can use the Languages filter to select your preferred language and see matching caregivers.
